questions.question: A weld must be inspected inside a vessel where flammable vapors prohibit energized electrical equipment. The practical MT choice — and its main limitation — is:
options: An AC yoke, limited only by extension-cord length
A permanent-magnet yoke: no power needed, but the field is fixed and cannot be switched off, making application, removal, and control harder
A prod kit, because prods cannot spark
A wet horizontal bench, which is explosion-proof by design
questions.explanation: Permanent-magnet yokes need no electrical power, which suits flammable atmospheres — prods and energized yokes are arc and spark sources. The trade is a fixed-strength field that cannot be turned off.

questions.question: Before weld inspection at the pole spacing to be used, an AC electromagnetic yoke should demonstrate a lifting power of at least about:
options: 40 lb of steel
100 lb of steel
10 lb of steel
4.5 lb of any metal
questions.explanation: Typical procedure checks are about 10 lb for AC yokes and about 40 lb for DC yokes, performed at the pole spacing that will actually be used on the work.

questions.question: A shaft 30 in. long and 5 in. in diameter (L/D = 6) needs longitudinal magnetization using a 3-turn cable wrap (low fill factor, NI ≈ 45,000/(L/D)). The approximate current — and therefore the equipment class — is:
options: About 2,500 A per shot, calling for a mobile or stationary unit
About 750 A per shot, within any portable pack's range
About 7,500 A per shot, beyond all MT equipment
About 250 A per shot, achievable with a yoke
questions.explanation: NI ≈ 45,000/6 = 7,500 ampere-turns; with 3 turns that is roughly 2,500 A — beyond typical portable packs (≈ 1,500 A max) but routine for mobile and stationary units. Your written procedure governs the final value.

questions.question: What ultimately justifies trusting an automated MT line to accept parts without an inspector watching every shot?
options: The manufacturer's purchase certification for the machine
Instrumented monitoring of every process variable plus periodic system verification using known-defect or QQI-equipped test parts
Amperage higher than any manual bench can deliver
A fixed conveyor speed that never changes
questions.explanation: Automation replaces human judgment only when every variable a person would sense — delivered current, bath flow, lamp intensity — is monitored with alarms, and the whole system periodically proves it still finds known flaws.

questions.question: Multidirectional benches are most economically justified for:
options: One-off weld repairs in the field
High-volume, complex-geometry production parts, where two separate magnetize-bathe-view cycles would choke throughput
Parts arriving too hot for any wet bath
Nonferrous parts that need multiple field directions
questions.explanation: Multidirectional units pay for themselves on production runs of crankshafts, knuckles, and similar complex forgings — full-orientation coverage in one bath-and-view cycle. Simple or one-off work is handled fine by sequential shots, and MT never applies to nonferrous parts.

questions.question: A water-vehicle bath is observed beading and breaking into rivulets on parts instead of wetting them. The deficiency and the remedy are:
options: Too many particles — extend the settling time
Inadequate wetting agent — condition the bath until a continuous, water-break-free film forms
Bath too cold — heat it above 135 °F
Excess antifoam agent — add oil to compensate
questions.explanation: Water needs wetting agents to spread over steel; a film that beads or breaks leaves uninspected streaks. The water-break check verifies the film, and conditioning per the manufacturer's data restores it.

questions.question: A forged fitting arrives at the bench still at about 250 °F. The wet fluorescent bath:
options: Is fine — heat improves particle mobility
Should not be applied; cool the part below the bath's service limit (around 135 °F) or use a dry powder rated for the temperature
Needs double concentration to compensate for evaporation
Works normally if shot times are shortened
questions.explanation: Wet baths are generally limited to about 135 °F — hotter surfaces evaporate carrier, degrade fluorescent dye, and can flash water to steam. Hot-surface work is dry powder territory, with some powders rated to roughly 600 °F.

questions.question: UV-A intensity and ambient light readings are valid only when taken:
options: At the lamp face, once per week
At the examination surface with calibrated meters, re-verified at shift start or every 8 hours and after bulb changes or power interruptions
Anywhere inside the booth, once per month
Through the inspector's UV-blocking spectacles for realism
questions.explanation: Both readings are taken where the part actually sits, with in-calibration instruments, and repeated on the standard triggers — shift start or every 8 hours, bulb or lamp changes, repositioning, and power interruptions.

questions.question: Stepping from bright shop light into the darkened booth, an inspector should:
options: Begin evaluating at once — the eye adapts instantly
Pause for dark adaptation (commonly at least 1 minute) and wear UV-absorbing spectacles, which block stray UV, cut eye-fluid haze, and improve indication contrast
Wear dark sunglasses to force faster adaptation
Raise the booth's ambient light to 50 fc to help the eyes adjust
questions.explanation: The eye needs an adaptation pause before it can detect faint fluorescent indications, and UV-absorbing (not sunglass-dark) spectacles stop the inspector's own eye fluid from fluorescing into a milky haze.

questions.question: Compared with mercury-vapor UV lamps, LED UV-A sources:
options: Require an even longer warm-up period
Reach full output instantly and run cooler, but require certification and checks of beam uniformity as well as peak intensity
Emit mostly UV-B and need extra filtering
Cannot reach 1,000 µW/cm² at working distance
questions.explanation: LED UV-A lamps are instant-on and cool-running with no restrike delay, but their beams can be intense yet uneven — so current practices call for manufacturer certification and verification of coverage, not just a single peak reading.
